Bake at 180 degrees Celsius for 45-55 minutes until internal temperature reaches 71C.
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing.
tips: Do not over-mix the meat or it becomes dense and tough.
A free-form loaf on a sheet pan browns better than one in a loaf pan.

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Creamy chicken and vegetable filling baked under a flaky golden pastry crust.
instructions: Poach chicken in broth until cooked, shred, and reserve broth.
Dice potato and carrot, boil until just tender.
Melt butter, add onion, cook until soft; add flour and stir 1 minute; slowly add reserved broth and milk.
Add chicken, potatoes, carrots, and peas to the sauce; pour into a baking dish.
Top with puff pastry, brush with egg wash, and bake at 200C for 25-30 minutes until golden.
tips: Vent the pastry with small cuts to let steam escape.
The filling should be thick enough to hold its shape when served.

name: Chili Con Carne
description: Spiced ground beef and bean stew with tomatoes, peppers, and warm spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add diced onion and bell pepper; sauté until soft.
Add garlic and ground beef; break up and brown.
Add chili powder, cumin, and paprika; cook 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes and drained kidney beans; bring to a simmer.
Cook uncovered for 30 minutes until thick. Serve with rice, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
tips: Chili improves significantly after a day — make ahead when possible.
A small square of dark chocolate adds surprising depth to the chili.

name: Fish Tacos
description: Crispy battered fish in warm tortillas with cabbage slaw and chipotle mayo.
instructions: Mix flour, cornstarch, and salt; whisk in beer until smooth batter forms.
Cut fish into strips, dip in batter, and fry in hot oil until golden and crispy.
Shred cabbage finely, toss with lime juice and salt.
Mix mayo with chipotle sauce for the drizzle.
Warm tortillas, fill with fish, cabbage slaw, chipotle mayo, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Use a firm white fish like cod or mahi-mahi that holds up to frying.
Double-layer the corn tortillas to prevent tearing.

name: Shrimp Scampi
description: Sautéed shrimp in a garlic butter and white wine sauce over linguine.
instructions: Cook linguine until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
Heat olive oil and half the butter; sauté minced garlic and chili flakes for 30 seconds.
Add shrimp in a single layer, cook 2 minutes per side until pink.
Add wine and lemon juice, simmer 2 minutes, then add remaining butter.
Toss with pasta and a splash of pasta water; garnish with parsley.
tips: Do not overcook shrimp — they turn rubbery in seconds past being done.
The wine sauce should be silky — pasta water helps emulsify it.

name: Pot Roast
description: Slow-braised beef chuck roast with root vegetables in a rich gravy.
instructions: Season beef with salt and pepper; sear on all sides in a hot Dutch oven with oil until deeply browned.
Remove beef, add onion and garlic; sauté until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 1 minute.
Return beef, add broth and thyme; bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Add potatoes and carrots, cover, and cook another 45-60 minutes until vegetables are tender and beef falls apart.
tips: Searing the meat deeply on all sides is what gives the gravy its rich flavor.
Do not open the oven frequently — trapped steam is doing the cooking.

name: Cornbread
description: Golden, slightly sweet cornbread baked in a cast iron skillet.
instructions: Preheat oven to 200C with a cast iron skillet inside.
Mix cornmeal, flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt.
Whisk buttermilk, egg, and most of the melted butter; combine with dry ingredients.
Brush hot skillet with remaining butter, pour batter in — it should sizzle.
Bake 18-22 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
tips: Preheating the skillet gives the cornbread a crispy, golden crust.
Do not over-mix — stir just until combined.

name: Baked Beans
description: Slow-cooked navy beans in a sweet and smoky tomato-molasses sauce.
instructions: Preheat oven to 175C.
Mix drained beans with ketchup, molasses, mustard, Worcestershire, paprika, diced onion, and salt.
Pour into a baking dish, cover with foil.
Bake for 45 minutes, remove foil, bake 15 more minutes until bubbly and thickened.
Serve as a side with BBQ meats or sandwiches.
tips: The sauce thickens significantly as it bakes.
Add diced bacon for a traditional smoky variation.

name: Sweet Potato Fries
description: Crispy baked sweet potato fries seasoned with paprika and garlic.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220C.
Cut sweet potatoes into even sticks about 1cm thick.
Toss with olive oil, cornstarch, paprika, garlic powder, and salt.
Spread in a single layer on a parchment-lined baking sheet — do not overcrowd.
Bake 25-30 minutes, flipping halfway, until crispy and golden.
tips: Cornstarch is the secret to crispy baked fries.
A single layer without crowding is essential — use two trays if needed.

name: Garden Salad
description: Fresh mixed greens with cucumber, tomato, and a light vinaigrette.
instructions: Wash and dry mixed greens; place in a bowl.
Slice cucumber, halve tomatoes, thinly slice red onion; add to greens.
Whisk olive oil, vinegar, salt, and pepper into a quick vinaigrette.
Toss salad with dressing just before serving.
tips: Dry greens thoroughly — wet leaves dilute the dressing.
Dress the salad at the last moment to keep it crisp.

name: Buffalo Wings
description: Crispy oven-baked chicken wings tossed in a tangy hot sauce and butter glaze, served with celery and blue cheese dip.
instructions: Pat wings completely dry with paper towels — this is essential for crispiness.
Toss wings with baking powder, salt, pepper, and garlic powder.
Arrange in a single layer on a wire rack over a baking sheet.
Bake at 220 degrees Celsius for 40-45 minutes, flipping halfway, until skin is golden and crispy.
Melt butter, mix with hot sauce, and toss the baked wings in the sauce. Serve with celery sticks and blue cheese dressing.
tips: Baking powder draws moisture out of the skin, making oven-baked wings as crispy as fried ones.
Pat wings bone-dry before seasoning — any moisture prevents crisping.

name: Fried Rice
description: Classic Chinese wok-fried rice with egg, vegetables, and soy sauce.
instructions: Heat oil in a wok over high heat until smoking.
Push oil to sides, scramble eggs in the center, then break into small pieces.
Add garlic, diced carrot, and peas; stir-fry for 2 minutes.
Add cold day-old rice, break up clumps, and toss vigorously on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, sesame oil, salt, and pepper; garnish with chopped spring onion.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — fresh rice is too moist and becomes mushy.
High heat and constant tossing prevent the rice from steaming.

name: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Spicy Sichuan stir-fry of chicken, peanuts, and dried chilies in a savory-sweet sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into cubes, marinate with 1 tbsp soy sauce and cornstarch for 15 minutes.
Mix remaining soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil into a sauce.
Heat oil in a wok, fry Sichuan peppercorns and dried chilies until fragrant.
Add chicken and stir-fry on high heat until cooked through, about 4-5 minutes.
Add garlic, ginger, peanuts, and the sauce; toss until glazed, garnish with spring onion.
tips: Toast the peanuts separately for extra crunch.
Sichuan peppercorns give the signature numbing sensation — do not skip them.

name: Sweet and Sour Chicken
description: Crispy battered chicken pieces coated in a tangy pineapple and bell pepper sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, coat in a batter of egg, cornstarch, and salt.
Deep fry in batches until golden and crispy; drain and set aside.
In a clean wok, stir-fry onion and bell pepper for 2 minutes.
Add ketchup, vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and pineapple; cook until sauce thickens.
Toss in the fried chicken, coat quickly, and serve immediately.
tips: Add the chicken to the sauce at the very last moment to keep it crispy.
Double-frying the chicken gives extra crunch.

name: Mapo Tofu
description: Silken tofu in a fiery Sichuan sauce with minced pork and fermented bean paste.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2cm cubes, blanch in salted water for 2 minutes; drain gently.
Heat oil, brown the minced pork until crispy.
Add doubanjiang, garlic, and ginger; stir-fry for 1 minute until fragrant and oil turns red.
Add broth, soy sauce, and tofu; simmer gently for 5 minutes without stirring.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry, sprinkle ground Sichuan peppercorn and spring onion.
tips: Handle tofu gently — it breaks easily.
Blanching tofu in salted water firms it up and removes the raw bean taste.

name: Chow Mein
description: Stir-fried egg noodles with vegetables and soy sauce, tossed in a hot wok.
instructions: Cook egg noodles according to package, drain, and toss with a drizzle of sesame oil.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat, add garlic and stir-fry shredded cabbage and carrot for 2 minutes.
Add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; toss vigorously for 2-3 minutes.
Add bean sprouts and spring onion in the last 30 seconds.
Serve immediately while noodles are hot and slightly charred.
tips: High heat and quick tossing give the signature wok hei (breath of the wok).
Do not overcook bean sprouts — they should stay crunchy.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
description: Tangy and spicy Chinese soup with tofu, mushrooms, and egg ribbons.
instructions: Soak dried mushrooms in hot water for 15 minutes; slice all mushrooms and tofu into strips.
Bring broth to a boil, add mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu; simmer 5 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper.
Stir in cornstarch slurry to thicken, then slowly drizzle beaten egg while stirring gently.
Finish with sesame oil and chili oil; garnish with spring onion.
tips: Add vinegar at the end to preserve its sharp tang.
Drizzle the egg slowly in a thin stream for delicate ribbons.

name: Dan Dan Noodles
description: Sichuan wheat noodles in a spicy, nutty sesame and chili oil sauce with minced pork.
instructions: Cook noodles according to package; drain.
Brown minced pork in oil until crispy.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, garlic, and Sichuan pepper into a sauce.
Divide sauce among bowls, add a splash of noodle cooking water, then top with noodles.
Top with crispy pork, crushed peanuts, and sliced spring onion; mix before eating.
tips: The sauce goes in the bowl first, then hot noodle water loosens it before adding noodles.
Adjust chili oil quantity to your spice tolerance.

name: Steamed Dumplings (Jiaozi)
description: Chinese dumplings filled with pork and cabbage, steamed until translucent.
instructions: Mix flour with boiling water, knead into a smooth dough, rest 30 minutes.
Finely chop cabbage, salt it, squeeze out moisture; mix with pork, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion.
Roll dough into small circles, place a spoonful of filling in center, pleat edges to seal.
Steam dumplings in a lined steamer for 10-12 minutes until wrapper is translucent.
Serve with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chili oil.
tips: Squeeze all water from the cabbage to prevent soggy dumplings.
Using boiling water for the dough makes wrappers more pliable.

name: Teriyaki Chicken
description: Glazed chicken thighs in a sweet and savory soy-mirin sauce, served over rice.
instructions: Score chicken skin lightly, season with salt.
Heat oil in a pan, cook chicken skin-side down for 5-6 minutes until crispy.
Flip and cook 3-4 more minutes until cooked through; remove from pan.
In the same pan, add soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and ginger; simmer until thick and glossy.
Slice chicken, place over rice, drizzle with teriyaki glaze, sprinkle sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: Cook the sauce until it coats the back of a spoon — it should be thick and glossy.
Scoring the chicken skin prevents it from curling during cooking.

name: Miso Soup
description: Traditional Japanese soup with dashi broth, miso paste, tofu, and wakame seaweed.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a gentle simmer — do not boil.
Soak dried wakame in water for 5 minutes; drain.
Cut tofu into small cubes.
Dissolve miso paste in a ladle of warm dashi, then stir back into the pot.
Add tofu and wakame, warm through for 1 minute; garnish with sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso soup after adding the paste — boiling destroys the beneficial enzymes and dulls the flavor.
Use white miso for a milder flavor, red miso for a deeper, saltier taste.

name: Sushi Bowl
description: Deconstructed sushi with seasoned rice, fresh fish, avocado, and edamame in a bowl.
instructions: Cook sushi rice, then season with rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt while still warm.
Slice salmon, avocado, and cucumber into thin pieces.
Divide seasoned rice among bowls.
Arrange salmon, avocado, cucumber, and edamame on top.
Tear nori into strips, sprinkle with sesame seeds, serve with soy sauce and pickled ginger.
tips: Fan the rice while seasoning to give it a glossy sheen.
Use the freshest sashimi-grade fish for raw preparations.

name: Ramen
description: Rich pork broth noodle soup with soft-boiled egg, chashu pork, and toppings.
instructions: For chashu: braise pork belly in soy sauce, mirin, garlic, and ginger at 160C for 1.5 hours until tender; slice.
Soft-boil eggs for 6.5 minutes, peel, and marinate in chashu braising liquid for 30 minutes.
Heat broth with remaining braising liquid, garlic, and ginger; simmer 15 minutes, strain.
Cook ramen noodles according to package, drain, and place in bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles, top with sliced chashu, halved egg, nori, and spring onion.
tips: A 6.5-minute egg gives a perfectly jammy yolk for ramen.
The broth should be piping hot when poured over the noodles.

name: Gyoza
description: Pan-fried Japanese dumplings with a crispy bottom and juicy pork-cabbage filling.
instructions: Finely chop cabbage, salt, squeeze out water. Mix with pork, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion.
Place a teaspoon of filling in each wrapper, fold in half and pleat one side to seal.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan, arrange gyoza in a circle flat-side down.
Fry until bottoms are golden, about 2 minutes, then add water and cover immediately.
Steam for 5 minutes until water evaporates and bottoms crisp up again; serve with ponzu sauce.
tips: The steam-then-fry technique gives gyoza their signature crispy bottom.
Pleating only one side creates the classic crescent shape.

name: Katsu Curry
description: Crispy breaded chicken cutlet served with Japanese curry sauce over rice.
instructions: Pound chicken to even thickness, season, coat in flour, beaten egg, then panko.
Deep fry at 170C for 5-6 minutes until golden and cooked through; drain and rest.
Sauté sliced onion, carrot, and potato in a pot for 5 minutes.
Add water, bring to a boil, simmer until vegetables are tender, then add curry roux; stir until thickened.
Slice the katsu, serve over rice with curry sauce poured alongside.
tips: Press the panko firmly onto the chicken for a thick, even crust.
Let curry roux dissolve off the heat for a smoother sauce.

name: Edamame
description: Steamed young soybeans in the pod, sprinkled with sea salt.
instructions: Bring water to a boil with a pinch of salt.
Add frozen edamame and cook for 3-4 minutes until tender.
Drain and toss with sea salt.
Serve warm or at room temperature as an appetizer.
tips: Squeeze the pods to pop the beans directly into your mouth.
A sprinkle of chili flakes or garlic salt adds variety.

name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl with seasoned vegetables, egg, beef, and gochujang sauce.
instructions: Marinate sliced beef in soy sauce, garlic, and sesame oil for 15 minutes; stir-fry until cooked.
Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately; season each with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot and zucchini; sauté each separately until just tender.
Place rice in bowls, arrange all toppings in sections on top, fry an egg sunny-side up for each bowl.
Serve with gochujang on the side; mix everything together before eating.
tips: Each vegetable should be cooked and seasoned separately for distinct flavors.
For dolsot bibimbap, use a hot stone bowl to get crispy rice at the bottom.

name: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: Spicy Korean fried rice made with aged kimchi, gochujang, and topped with a fried egg.
instructions: Roughly chop kimchi, reserving the juice.
Heat oil in a wok, stir-fry kimchi with its juice for 2-3 minutes.
Add cold rice, gochujang, and soy sauce; toss on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a separate pan.
Plate rice, top with fried egg, sprinkle sesame seeds and spring onion; break yolk before eating.
tips: Aged, sour kimchi works best — fresh kimchi lacks the depth of flavor.
The runny egg yolk mixed into the rice creates a rich, creamy sauce.

name: Korean BBQ Beef (Bulgogi)
description: Thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet soy-pear sauce, grilled or pan-fried.
instructions: Freeze beef for 30 minutes for easier slicing, then slice paper-thin against the grain.
Blend pear, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger into a marinade.
Marinate beef for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for best results.
Cook on a hot grill or pan in batches over high heat for 1-2 minutes per side.
Garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion; serve with rice and lettuce wraps.
tips: Pear is a natural meat tenderizer and adds subtle sweetness.
Do not overcrowd the pan — cook in a single layer for proper searing.

name: Japchae
description: Korean stir-fried sweet potato glass noodles with vegetables and sesame.
instructions: Cook sweet potato noodles in boiling water for 6-7 minutes until chewy; drain and cut shorter with scissors.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil and salt.
Stir-fry each vegetable separately in a little oil until just tender.
Toss noodles with soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and garlic.
Combine everything in a large bowl, add sesame seeds, and serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately preserves their individual textures.
These noodles are chewy by nature — do not overcook them.

name: Kimchi
description: Korean fermented napa cabbage with gochugaru, garlic, ginger, and fish sauce.
instructions: Quarter cabbage, salt generously between leaves, and let sit 2 hours; rinse and drain.
Blend garlic, ginger, fish sauce, sugar, and a splash of water into a paste; mix with gochugaru.
Julienne daikon and cut spring onion into 2-inch pieces.
Rub the paste thoroughly between each cabbage leaf, adding daikon and spring onion.
Pack tightly into a jar, leave at room temperature for 1-3 days, then refrigerate.
tips: The cabbage should be wilted but still crunchy after salting.
Ferment longer in cool weather, shorter in warm weather; taste daily.

name: Pad Thai
description: Thailand's iconic stir-fried rice noodles with tamarind sauce, shrimp, peanuts, and lime.
instructions: Soak rice noodles in warm water for 20 minutes until pliable; drain.
Mix tamarind paste, fish sauce, and sugar into the pad thai sauce.
Heat oil in a wok, fry cubed tofu until golden, then add shrimp and cook until pink.
Push everything to one side, scramble eggs on the other, then add noodles and sauce; toss vigorously.
Add bean sprouts and chives in the last 30 seconds; serve with crushed peanuts, lime wedge, and chili flakes.
tips: Soak noodles, do not boil them — they finish cooking in the wok.
The wok must be very hot for authentic pad thai flavor.

name: Thai Green Curry
description: Aromatic coconut-based curry with green chili paste, Thai basil, and vegetables.
instructions: Heat oil, fry green curry paste for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk; stir-fry with the paste for 2 minutes.
Add chicken or tofu, cook for 3-4 minutes until sealed.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add eggplant, bamboo shoots, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, and sugar; simmer 10 minutes.
Stir in Thai basil, top with sliced red chili, and serve with jasmine rice.
tips: Frying the curry paste in coconut cream first releases its full aroma.
Fish sauce, not salt, is the primary seasoning in Thai curries.

name: Tom Yum Soup
description: Spicy and sour Thai soup with shrimp, lemongrass, galangal, and lime.
instructions: Bruise lemongrass stalks, slice galangal and tear lime leaves.
Bring broth to a boil with lemongrass, galangal, lime leaves, and crushed chilies.
Add halved mushrooms and tomatoes; simmer 5 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook until pink, about 2-3 minutes.
Turn off heat, add fish sauce, lime juice, and sugar; garnish with coriander.
tips: Add lime juice off the heat to preserve its bright flavor.
The aromatics (lemongrass, galangal, lime leaves) are for flavor — not meant to be eaten.

name: Thai Basil Chicken (Pad Krapao)
description: Quick stir-fry of minced chicken with holy basil, chili, and garlic over rice.
instructions: Pound garlic and chilies in a mortar into a rough paste.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, add the garlic-chili paste and stir-fry 30 seconds.
Add minced chicken, break up, and cook on high heat until browned.
Season with soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and sugar; toss well.
Stir in basil leaves until just wilted; serve over rice with a crispy fried egg on top.
tips: Holy basil is traditional — it has a peppery, clove-like flavor different from sweet basil.
The wok must be screaming hot for authentic Thai street food flavor.

name: Massaman Curry
description: Rich Thai curry with tender beef, potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices in coconut milk.
instructions: Cut beef into cubes, quarter potatoes and onion.
Heat thick coconut cream in a pot, fry curry paste for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add beef and sear in the paste for 3-4 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add potatoes, onion, cinnamon, and bay leaves; simmer for 40-50 minutes until beef is tender.
Season with fish sauce, tamarind, palm sugar, and peanuts; serve with jasmine rice.
tips: Massaman is the mildest Thai curry — perfect for those who prefer less heat.
The curry improves significantly the next day as flavors meld.

name: Pho
description: Vietnamese aromatic beef broth noodle soup with rare beef, herbs, and bean sprouts.
instructions: Char onion and ginger under a broiler until blackened; this adds smoky depth.
Simmer broth with charred onion, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and fish sauce for 45 minutes; strain.
Cook rice noodles according to package; place in bowls.
Arrange raw sliced beef on top of the noodles.
Pour piping hot broth over the beef (it cooks instantly); serve with bean sprouts, basil, lime, hoisin, and sriracha.
tips: Slice the beef paper-thin — freezing it for 20 minutes makes this easier.
The broth must be boiling hot to cook the raw beef when poured over.

name: Banh Mi
description: Vietnamese crusty baguette sandwich with pate, pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs.
instructions: Julienne daikon and carrot, pickle in rice vinegar, sugar, and salt for 15 minutes.
Slice baguettes lengthwise, toast lightly until crispy outside but soft inside.
Spread pate on one side and mayonnaise on the other.
Layer sliced meat, pickled vegetables, cucumber slices, cilantro, and sliced jalapeno.
Press closed and serve immediately.
tips: The baguette should be crusty but light — Vietnamese bakeries make a distinctive airy bread.
The contrast of rich pate, tangy pickles, and fresh herbs is what makes banh mi special.

name: Vietnamese Spring Rolls
description: Fresh rice paper rolls filled with shrimp, vermicelli, herbs, and vegetables.
instructions: Cook rice vermicelli, drain, and rinse with cold water.
Julienne cucumber and carrot, halve the shrimp lengthwise.
Dip rice paper in warm water for 10 seconds until pliable.
Layer lettuce, vermicelli, vegetables, herbs, and shrimp on the wrapper; fold sides in and roll tightly.
Serve with peanut dipping sauce (peanut butter, hoisin, sriracha, lime juice, and warm water).
tips: Do not over-soak the rice paper — it should be slightly stiff and will continue softening.
Roll tightly for a neat presentation; practice makes perfect.

name: Bun Cha
description: Vietnamese grilled pork meatballs and sliced pork belly with rice noodles and dipping broth.
instructions: Mix ground pork with 1 tbsp fish sauce, garlic, shallot, and 1 tsp sugar; shape into small meatballs.
Marinate pork belly slices in fish sauce, sugar, and garlic.
Grill meatballs and pork belly over high heat until charred and cooked through.
Make dipping broth: warm water, fish sauce, sugar, rice vinegar, and sliced chili.
Serve grilled meats in the dipping broth alongside dry rice noodles, lettuce, herbs, and pickled carrot.
tips: The dipping broth should be sweet, sour, and salty in balance.
Charring the meat over flame is essential for the smoky flavor.

name: Stir-Fried Tofu with Vegetables
description: Crispy pan-fried tofu tossed with mixed vegetables in a savory garlic-soy sauce.
instructions: Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove moisture, cut into cubes, and toss with cornstarch.
Pan-fry tofu in oil until golden and crispy on all sides; remove and set aside.
In the same wok, stir-fry broccoli, bell pepper, and snap peas for 3 minutes.
Add garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; cook 1 minute.
Return tofu, toss with sesame oil, and serve over rice.
tips: Pressing the tofu is essential — wet tofu will not crisp up.
The cornstarch coating gives tofu a satisfying crunch.

name: Falafel
description: Crispy deep-fried chickpea fritters seasoned with herbs, cumin, and coriander.
instructions: Drain soaked chickpeas (do not use canned) and pulse in a food processor with onion, garlic, and herbs until coarse but holds together.
Mix in cumin, coriander, salt, pepper, and baking powder.
Refrigerate mixture for 30 minutes, then shape into small patties or balls.
Deep fry in 180C oil for 3-4 minutes until deep golden and crispy.
Serve in pita with tahini sauce, pickles, and fresh salad.
tips: Never use canned chickpeas — they are too wet and falafel will fall apart.
Soaking dried chickpeas overnight is essential for the right texture.

name: Chicken Shawarma
description: Warmly spiced chicken thighs roasted with cumin, paprika, and turmeric, sliced thin.
instructions: Combine yogurt, all spices, garlic, lemon juice, olive oil, and salt into a marinade.
Coat chicken thighs thoroughly, marinate for at least 1 hour (overnight is best).
Bake at 220C for 20-25 minutes, or grill until charred on edges and cooked through.
Let rest 5 minutes, then slice thin against the grain.
Serve in warm pita with pickled turnips, tahini sauce, and fresh salad.
tips: Chicken thighs stay juicier than breast for shawarma.
Broiling for the last 2-3 minutes gives the signature charred edges.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh Lebanese salad of finely chopped parsley, bulgur wheat, tomato, and lemon.
instructions: Soak bulgur in hot water for 15 minutes until soft; drain and squeeze out excess water.
Finely chop parsley, mint, tomatoes, and spring onion.
Combine bulgur with the chopped herbs and vegetables.
Dress with lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper; toss well.
Serve at room temperature — tabbouleh should be predominantly green with parsley.
tips: Parsley is the star, not the bulgur — use far more parsley than grain.
Squeeze excess water from the bulgur to prevent a soggy salad.

name: Baba Ganoush
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
instructions: Roast whole eggplants directly over a gas flame or under a broiler until skin is charred and flesh is collapsed.
Peel off charred skin, drain the flesh for 10 minutes to remove excess liquid.
Mash or pulse eggplant flesh with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt.
Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle paprika and parsley.
Serve with warm pita bread or raw vegetables.
tips: Charring over an open flame gives the signature smoky flavor.
Draining the eggplant prevents a watery dip.

name: Middle Eastern Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a rich tomato and bell pepper sauce seasoned with cumin and harissa.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft.
Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and harissa; cook for 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until thick.
Make wells, crack eggs into the sauce, cover, and cook 5-7 minutes until whites are set.
Crumble feta over top, garnish with parsley, serve with crusty bread.
tips: The sauce should be thick before adding eggs — too thin and eggs sink to the bottom.
Cover the pan to cook egg whites from the top as well.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb shaped onto skewers and grilled until charred and juicy.
instructions: Grate onion, squeeze out excess moisture.
Mix lamb with grated onion, garlic, parsley, all spices, salt, and pepper; knead for 2 minutes.
Shape mixture onto flat skewers in long oval shapes.
Grill over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred but still juicy inside.
Serve with pita, hummus, tahini sauce, and fresh salad.
tips: Kneading the meat mixture well binds it so it holds on the skewer.
Squeezing moisture from the onion prevents the kofta from falling apart.

name: Fattoush
description: Levantine bread salad with crispy pita chips, vegetables, and a tangy sumac dressing.
instructions: Tear or cut pita into pieces, toast or fry until crispy and golden.
Chop lettuce, cucumber, tomatoes, radishes, spring onion, mint, and parsley.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, pomegranate molasses, and salt into a dressing.
Toss vegetables with dressing, then add crispy pita just before serving.
Serve immediately — the pita should be crunchy, not soggy.
tips: Add pita at the very last moment to maintain crunch.
Sumac provides the signature tangy, lemony flavor.

name: Mujaddara
description: Middle Eastern comfort dish of lentils and rice topped with crispy caramelized onions.
instructions: Cook lentils in water for 15 minutes until half-tender.
Add washed rice, cumin, cinnamon, and salt to the lentils; cook on low heat for 15 minutes until both are tender.
While rice cooks, thinly slice all onions and fry in olive oil on medium-low heat for 20-25 minutes until deeply caramelized and crispy.
Fluff the rice and lentils with a fork.
Serve topped generously with crispy fried onions and a side of yogurt.
tips: The crispy onions are the soul of this dish — do not rush them.
Cook onions on medium-low heat for deep caramelization, not burning.

name: Labneh
description: Thick strained yogurt served as a creamy dip with olive oil and za'atar.
instructions: Mix yogurt with salt.
Place in a cheesecloth-lined strainer over a bowl; refrigerate for 12-24 hours to drain whey.
Transfer thick labneh to a plate, spread into a swirl.
Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle za'atar and mint.
Serve with warm pita bread or fresh vegetables.
tips: The longer you strain, the thicker the labneh — 24 hours gives a cream cheese consistency.
Full-fat yogurt is essential; low-fat versions produce a grainy texture.

name: Lamb Shawarma Wrap
description: Thin-sliced spiced lamb wrapped in flatbread with pickles, tahini, and fresh vegetables.
instructions: Slice lamb thin, marinate in yogurt, all spices, garlic, and salt for at least 2 hours.
Cook lamb in a hot pan or grill in batches until charred and cooked through.
Warm flatbreads on a hot pan for 30 seconds each side.
Layer lamb, lettuce, sliced tomato, and pickled turnips on the flatbread.
Drizzle with tahini, roll tightly, and serve.
tips: Marinating overnight yields the most flavorful shawarma.
Cook lamb in small batches on very high heat for proper charring.

name: Muhammara
description: Syrian roasted red pepper and walnut dip with pomegranate molasses and cumin.
instructions: Toast walnuts in a dry pan for 3-4 minutes until fragrant.
Pulse roasted red peppers, walnuts, and breadcrumbs in a food processor until coarse.
Add pomegranate molasses, cumin, chili flakes, lemon juice, and salt; pulse to combine.
Drizzle in olive oil while pulsing until desired consistency.
Serve drizzled with more olive oil and a sprinkle of walnuts.
tips: The texture should be coarse, not smooth like hummus.
Pomegranate molasses gives the distinctive sweet-tart flavor.

name: Chicken Kabob
description: Juicy marinated chicken pieces threaded onto skewers and grilled over charcoal.
instructions: Bloom saffron in 1 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes.
Mix yogurt, lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, grated onion, saffron water, and all spices.
Cut chicken into 2-inch cubes, coat in marinade, refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Thread onto skewers and grill over high heat for 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve with saffron rice, grilled tomatoes, and warm flatbread.
tips: Grated onion in the marinade tenderizes the chicken and adds moisture.
Do not overcook — chicken thighs should be juicy, not dry.

name: Koshari
description: Egyptian national dish of rice, lentils, and pasta topped with spiced tomato sauce and crispy onions.
instructions: Cook lentils in water for 15 minutes until almost tender, then add rice and cook together until done.
Cook pasta separately until al dente; drain.
Thinly slice onions and fry in oil until deeply caramelized and crispy; set aside.
For tomato sauce: sauté garlic, add canned tomatoes, vinegar, cumin, chili, and salt; simmer 10 minutes.
Layer rice-lentils, then pasta, top with tomato sauce and crispy fried onions.
tips: The crispy fried onions are essential — they provide crunch and sweetness.
Each component is simple, but layered together they create a satisfying dish.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Dolma)
description: Tender grape leaves filled with herbed rice, pine nuts, and lemon.
instructions: Rinse grape leaves and pat dry; if very salty, blanch briefly.
Mix raw rice with finely chopped onion, herbs, pine nuts, 1 tbsp olive oil, tomato paste, allspice, and salt.
Place a spoonful of filling on each grape leaf, fold sides in, and roll tightly.
Arrange rolls tightly in a pot, seam-side down, drizzle with remaining olive oil and lemon juice.
Add water, weigh down with a plate, cover, and simmer on low heat for 45-50 minutes until rice is cooked.
tips: Pack the rolls tightly in the pot so they hold their shape during cooking.
Serve at room temperature for the best flavor.

name: Manakish
description: Lebanese flatbread topped with za'atar and olive oil, baked until golden.
instructions: Divide dough into 4 pieces, roll each into a thin round.
Mix za'atar with olive oil and a pinch of salt into a paste.
Spread the za'atar mixture evenly over each dough round.
Bake at 230C for 8-10 minutes until dough is golden and crispy on edges.
Serve warm, folded with labneh, tomatoes, and fresh mint.
tips: The dough should be thin for a crispy result.
Za'atar and olive oil should be generously applied.

name: Oatmeal
description: Warm, creamy cooked oats topped with banana, cinnamon, and honey.
instructions: Bring milk or water to a simmer with a pinch of salt.
Add oats and cook on low heat for 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally.
The oatmeal is done when creamy and the oats are tender.
Top with sliced banana, a drizzle of honey, and a sprinkle of cinnamon.
tips: A pinch of salt brings out the natural sweetness of oats.
For creamier oatmeal, use a mix of milk and water.

name: Hard Boiled Eggs
description: Perfectly cooked hard-boiled eggs with a fully set but not chalky yolk.
instructions: Place eggs in a single layer in a pot, cover with cold water by 1 inch.
Add salt, bring to a rolling boil over high heat.
Once boiling, remove from heat, cover, and let sit for exactly 10 minutes.
Transfer eggs to an ice bath for 5 minutes to stop cooking.
Peel under running water and serve whole, halved, or sliced.
tips: Starting in cold water and using residual heat prevents rubbery whites.
Older eggs peel more easily than fresh ones.

name: Tomato Soup
description: Silky smooth roasted tomato soup finished with a swirl of cream and fresh basil.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion until soft, add garlic and cook 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, broth, sugar, salt, and pepper; simmer for 20 minutes.
Blend until smooth using an immersion blender or regular blender.
Stir in cream, adjust seasoning.
Serve with torn basil and crusty bread for dunking.
tips: A touch of sugar balances the acidity of the tomatoes.
For a vegan version, skip the cream or use coconut cream.

name: Grilled Cheese Sandwich
description: Golden, buttery toasted bread with melted gooey cheese inside.
instructions: Butter one side of each bread slice.
Place one slice butter-side down in a pan, add sliced or grated cheese.
Top with second slice, butter-side up.
Cook on medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cheese is melted.
tips: Medium-low heat is key — too hot and the bread burns before cheese melts.
A mix of cheeses (cheddar + mozzarella) gives the best flavor and stretch.

name: Pasta with Pesto
description: Al dente pasta tossed with fresh homemade basil pesto and parmesan.
instructions: Cook pasta in salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
Blend basil, pine nuts, garlic, and parmesan in a food processor; stream in olive oil.
Toss drained pasta with pesto and a splash of pasta water until coated.
Serve with extra parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil.
tips: Do not cook the pesto — it should stay raw and vibrant green.
Pasta water helps emulsify the sauce and makes it cling to the pasta.

name: Oven Roasted Chicken
description: Whole chicken roasted with herbs and garlic until skin is crispy and meat is juicy.
instructions: Pat chicken completely dry with paper towels — this is the most important step for crispy skin.
Rub softened butter mixed with minced garlic, salt, and pepper under and over the skin.
Stuff the cavity with lemon halves, thyme, and rosemary.
Truss the legs, drizzle with olive oil, and place in a roasting pan.
Roast at 200C for 1 hour 15 minutes until skin is golden and internal temperature reaches 74C; rest 15 minutes before carving.
tips: A dry surface is the number one secret to crispy skin.
Resting for 15 minutes lets juices redistribute — do not skip this.

name: Vegetable Stir-Fry
description: Quick mixed vegetable stir-fry in a savory soy-garlic sauce, served over rice.
instructions: Cut all vegetables into bite-sized pieces of similar thickness.
Mix soy sauce, sesame oil, and cornstarch with 2 tbsp water for the sauce.
Heat oil in a wok or large pan over high heat.
Add garlic and ginger, then vegetables starting with hardest (broccoli, carrot) first.
Add sauce, toss for 1-2 minutes until vegetables are crisp-tender and glazed; serve over rice.
tips: Prep everything before you start cooking — stir-frying moves fast.
Do not overcook — vegetables should be bright and still have a slight crunch.

name: Banana Bread
description: Moist, tender quick bread made with overripe bananas and a hint of cinnamon.
instructions: Mash bananas in a bowl until smooth with some lumps remaining.
Mix in melted butter, sugar, egg, and vanilla.
Fold in flour, baking soda, cinnamon, and salt until just combined.
Fold in walnuts if using, pour into a greased loaf pan.
Bake at 175C for 55-60 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean; cool in pan for 10 minutes.
tips: The riper and blacker the bananas, the better the bread — overripe is ideal.
Do not over-mix the batter or the bread becomes tough.

name: Chicken Noodle Soup
description: Comforting clear broth soup with tender chicken, egg noodles, and vegetables.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery until softened.
Add garlic, cook 30 seconds, then pour in chicken broth with bay leaf.
Add whole chicken breast, bring to a simmer, cook for 15 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Remove chicken, shred with two forks, return to pot.
Add egg noodles, cook for 6-8 minutes until tender; season and garnish with parsley.
tips: Cooking chicken in the broth adds flavor to both.
Add noodles near the end — they absorb broth and can become mushy if overcooked.

name: Fruit Smoothie
description: Quick blended fruit drink with yogurt, banana, and berries.
instructions: Add banana, frozen berries, yogurt, milk, and honey to a blender.
Blend on high for 30-60 seconds until smooth and creamy.
Adjust thickness with more milk if needed.
Pour into a glass and serve immediately.
tips: Using frozen fruit gives a thick, cold smoothie without needing ice.
Any combination of fruits works — experiment freely.

name: Lentil Soup
description: Hearty, warming red lentil soup with cumin, garlic, and a squeeze of lemon.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and carrot until soft.
Add garlic, cumin, and paprika; cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add washed lentils and broth; bring to a boil, then simmer for 20 minutes until lentils are soft.
Blend partially for a creamy-chunky texture, or fully for smooth soup.
Season with salt and lemon juice; serve with a drizzle of olive oil and crusty bread.
tips: Red lentils cook quickly and do not need soaking.
Lemon juice added at the end brightens the entire soup.

name: Mushroom Omelette
description: Fluffy folded omelette filled with sauteed mushrooms and melted cheese.
instructions: Sauté sliced mushrooms in half the butter until golden; set aside.
Beat eggs with salt and pepper until uniform.
Melt remaining butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat, pour in eggs.
Gently push cooked edges toward center, tilting pan to let raw egg flow to edges.
When almost set but still slightly wet on top, add mushrooms and cheese to one half; fold and slide onto plate.
tips: A French omelette should be pale yellow with no browning.
Slightly wet on top when folding — residual heat finishes cooking.

name: Spaghetti Bolognese
description: Rich Italian-style meat sauce with ground beef, tomatoes, and herbs over spaghetti.
instructions: Finely dice onion, carrot, and celery (soffritto).
Heat olive oil, sauté soffritto until soft, add garlic.
Add ground beef, break up and brown thoroughly.
Add wine, cook until reduced, then add tomatoes, tomato paste, and Italian seasoning.
Simmer on low for 30 minutes; serve over al dente spaghetti with parmesan.
tips: The soffritto (onion, carrot, celery) is the flavor base — cook it low and slow.
Simmer the sauce longer for deeper flavor — 30 minutes is the minimum.

name: Fried Egg
description: A perfectly fried egg with crispy edges and a runny yolk.
instructions: Heat butter or oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Crack egg into the pan gently.
Cook for 2-3 minutes until white is set and edges are slightly crispy.
Season with salt and pepper; serve immediately.
tips: Crack the egg into a small bowl first to avoid shell fragments.
For over-easy, flip gently and cook 30 seconds more.

name: Toast with Butter and Jam
description: Simple toasted bread with butter and fruit preserves.
instructions: Toast bread until golden and crispy.
Spread butter on warm toast so it melts.
Top with fruit jam of your choice.
Serve immediately with tea or coffee.
tips: Spread butter while the toast is hot for even melting.
Thick-cut artisan bread makes a more satisfying breakfast.
